as far as the phrase “citizen journalist,” this is not only a stupid phrase but one that delegitimizes the validity of the work that you are sourcing.

A “citizen journalist” is a person who was in a space as a regular person who then started to disseminate information incidentally.

In an environment where press freedoms are under attack, it is profoundly unhelpful to minimize indie press to “activist” or “citizen journalist.”

I identify as a movement journalist because I cover movements. I am a reporter because that is what I do: Take notes to disseminate information outward.

My reporting work is accessible because I don’t believe in paywalls, the information should be easy to disseminate, and because you deserve to know what’s going on as it happens.

Also: I’m doing what I have the financial capacity to do. If you want it more structured, you have to buy that!

THIS IS HOW JOURNALISM SHOULD BE

YOU FORGET THAT BECAUSE THEIR PROFIT MODELS RELY ON YOU FORGETTING THAT

I am intentionally doing legitimate work outside the confines of the mainstream because that mainstream frequently leans in favor of the people I am reporting. If I was doing this same  work at any mainstream outlet, it would still get friction because my work sabotages fascist disinfo & propaganda.

My work covering the far-right extremists active in New York City has been covered in mainstream spaces. Their specific identities may not be paired with that coverage, but their extremism, as reported by me, has been. CBS. New York Times. WNYC radio.

Even in the most generous sense of the term “activist,” I’m not an activist by way of the fact that I don’t *do* anything. Also, this is a term used to delegitimize independent reporting. Anything that’s not published in mainstream outlets gets cast aside as advocacy or activism. This is dangerous.

Apparently everyone on instagram is just now learning about the far-right extremists I have been reporting on for the last 5 years. And they’re referring to me as “an activist and citizen journalist” while doing it.

Your students were such a pleasure to spend time with. I learned so much from them!

Our conversation with Leah opened with each student introducing themselves and saying why they were interested in taking a class on this topic. What struck Leah was how "the far right" was not an abstraction for most students, but was instead a very familiar, even intimate, aspect of their lives.

For this piece I was able to track down a young progressive Oregonian who, in the 1960s, started attending John Birch Society meetings out of curiosity. He ended up getting assaulted at a 1963 right wing meeting in Eugene hosted by a future OR GOP chair. rightlandia.ghost.io/the-everyday...

One theme of the course was how "the far right" has become an object of curiosity and concern at different moments in US history. It happened in the 30s as fascist groups emerged across the US. It happened in the 60s when the John Birch Society was in the news. It happened after the OKC bombing.

This semester I taught a course on the History of the Far Right in the US. We were lucky to have one of the Pacific Northwest's best contemporary chroniclers and interpreters of the far right, @leahsottile.bsky.social, visit the class. This piece by Leah was inspired by what she heard students say.

I’m only 20 minutes by car (or Uber, on chemo days) from Derriford but Treliske is a full 90-minute drive. The biggest issue was working out how to do the bowel prep and get there for 7am for surgery. A friend picked me up the day before and gave me their spare bedroom/bathroom and lots of loo roll.

I liked the surgeon at Treliske (Truro) but not the ward or staffing conditions. Whereas Derriford seems very good with the whole oncology/chemo stuff. A friend who’s having chemo at Treliske was told they don’t offer the option of a cold cap because it takes too much time.

Almost the first thing she said was “this probably won’t cure you but it’s about giving you as long as possible before it comes back.” I guess it’s important to make it clear because it was such a big surgery that I’m sure at that point there’ll be women who decide just to do chemo.
